behaviors.description: The keyword-frequency analyzer assigns the correct sentiment when the review contains only words from the configured positive and negative word lists.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic local business logic with no external dependencies. The failure mode is incorrect matching or threshold calculation. Real configuration objects (word lists, thresholds) and test input serve as test data. A unit test directly exercises the matching algorithm and catches failures in keyword detection, threshold boundary cases, and scoring. Mocking the analyzer would hide bugs in the core algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Create test configuration with specific positive words (e.g., 'great', 'excellent'), negative words (e.g., 'bad', 'poor'), and a scoring threshold. Provide test reviews containing only positive words, only negative words, and exact threshold-boundary cases. Verify the returned sentiment score matches the configuration for each case.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level testing is sufficient. No external dependencies or non-determinism means a single test run will catch systematic failures.

behaviors.description: The explanation analyzer produces reasoning that references specific phrases from the original review, not generic boilerplate.
behaviors.justification: A mocked analyzer hides the real failure—we would hardcode specific phrases and the test would always pass. The description 'produces natural language reasoning about why the customer feels' suggests an LLM-based analyzer. If LLM-based, we must test with real inference to verify the model actually references specific phrases from the review and doesn't default to generic responses. Mocking the dependency guarantees false positives. If the analyzer is rule-based (less likely), Integration would suffice, but the LLM case dominates.
behaviors.test_description: Run the actual explanation analyzer (not mocked) with diverse real review text samples. Collect the explanation output and verify it contains identifiable phrases or paraphrases from the input review (not generic templates). Test with multiple distinct reviews (e.g., 'The app crashes frequently', 'Excellent customer support', 'Confusing interface') to ensure non-boilerplate responses. If LLM-based, run the same input multiple times to detect if non-determinism causes some runs to fall back to boilerplate.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic-level testing with real LLM inference is necessary. If the explanation is intermittently boilerplate due to LLM non-determinism, multiple runs will catch it. A single Integration test might get lucky.

behaviors.description: When a review contains mixed signals (both positive and negative keywords), the sentiment score reflects the configured tie-breaking rule.
behaviors.justification: Pure deterministic business logic. The failure mode is incorrect application of the tie-breaking rule (e.g., should favor negative but favors positive). Real configuration (tie-breaking rule, word lists) and test input (reviews with equal positive/negative keywords) serve as test data. Unit testing directly verifies the tie-breaking logic without any external dependencies or mocking.
behaviors.test_description: Create test configuration with a defined tie-breaking rule (e.g., 'favor negative', 'count majority', or 'require supermajority'). Generate test reviews with equal counts of positive and negative keywords, and reviews that test boundary cases of the rule. Verify the returned sentiment matches the configured tie-breaking behavior.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level testing is sufficient. The tie-breaking logic is deterministic and depends only on the algorithm and configuration, both controlled in the test.

behaviors.description: The explanation for a sarcastic review (e.g., 'Great, another broken feature') correctly identifies the negative intent despite positive surface words.
behaviors.justification: Sarcasm detection is a semantic and contextual task that cannot be reliably detected by keyword matching or simple rules. It requires real language understanding, which in production typically means an LLM or advanced NLP model. A mocked analyzer hides the failure completely—we'd hardcode the correct sarcasm interpretation. We must test the real analyzer with real inference on sarcastic examples to verify it interprets intent correctly. The failure mode is that the analyzer misses sarcasm and incorrectly classifies it as positive sentiment.
behaviors.test_description: Run the actual explanation analyzer with sarcastic review inputs (e.g., 'Great, another broken feature', 'Perfect, it only took three days to get a response', 'Amazing how often this crashes'). Verify the explanation output correctly identifies or infers negative intent, not positive. Include control examples (genuine positive reviews) to verify the analyzer doesn't over-correct. If the analyzer uses an LLM, run the same sarcastic inputs multiple times to verify consistency in intent detection.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic-level testing is necessary because sarcasm interpretation depends on real semantic understanding and LLM reasoning. A rule-based or mocked approach would miss this failure.

behaviors.description: Both outputs (score and explanation) are returned together in a single response, with the explanation referencing the same sentiment direction as the score.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires both the keyword analyzer and explanation analyzer to function correctly and produce aligned outputs. The keyword analyzer is deterministic (Unit-testable), but the explanation analyzer is likely LLM-based (Agentic). The failure mode is sentiment mismatch: score is positive but explanation implies negative (or vice versa). If we mock either analyzer, we hide real integration failures and LLM output quality issues. If the explanation analyzer is non-deterministic (likely, if LLM-based), a single Integration run might randomly align on one attempt but fail on another. We must test both together with real implementations, and account for LLM non-determinism.
behaviors.test_description: Run both analyzers end-to-end with diverse review inputs (clearly positive, clearly negative, mixed/sarcastic). Collect both the sentiment score and explanation output. Verify the explanation's implied sentiment direction matches the numerical score (positive explanation for positive score, negative explanation for negative score). Test with reviews of varying clarity and sentiment complexity. If the explanation analyzer is LLM-based, run the same input multiple times to detect if non-determinism occasionally produces misaligned outputs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic-level testing is appropriate because the explanation output may be non-deterministic (LLM-based), and misalignment can be intermittent. Integration testing alone might miss occasional failures due to LLM variance. Multiple runs with real inference catch both systematic and intermittent misalignment.
